This is how this game can be played. I'm going to be one of you guys, and I'm going to hear your stories, and then I'm going to try to ask a bunch of questions, and then I'm going to take like five minute break where I can brainstorm a couple things, and then I'm going to come up in character and be who Steven's picked. Have you told them they've picked yet? Nope. Oh, it's top secret. Except for many rives. Except for the rest of the fun. All right. Do you want to, well, should we, let's see. All right. I'm going to announce who special guest is for today. Holly. Holly, congratulations. Come on up. I'm going to be Holly today. You're coming up. Yes, you are. Oh, my gosh. You're coming up. This is for you. Okay. And you want to sit down. You want to stand. What's better for you? I'm standing. It's good. Okay. All right. You're going to make this easy on me, right? You can have a picture. You're going to picture together? Yeah. Beautiful. It worked. All right. Okay. All right. So this is how I play this game. And just so you guys know, like this is literally what we do every single time. It's us in front of whiteboard. I draw a line down the middle and this is where the game starts. When we did the program that most of you guys signed up for in March that eventually got to you here. The night before I was pitching it, this is what we did. We stood up here. Stephen was there. Dave was there. And this is the same game we played because I'm not very good. Pre-planning. So we just did it and there's ways that way. Okay. So first off, tell me. So I know this from last night and that's about all I know. So I'm really coming into this with not much knowing. So tell me about what you do. Okay. I'm a blogger. Okay. I run kidsactivities.com and the quirky mama Facebook page, which has 3.2 million fans. So it's fun thing to do with kids and research with your parents and the blogging team. And the basic underlying message that we're saying is it's more about having fun with your kids and pulling stuff out of the kitchen junk drawer and then going and buying a sense of anything. Okay. So is that on the craft side or all sorts of stuff? It's everything. So like a lot of it is twist on traditional childhood games and then simple crafts. Like we probably have 300 paper plate crafts and you know, things you can make with whatever you have at home already. But don't go buy anything. Cool. How long would you start it? I've been blogging for 10 years. Dang. What was the reason why you started initially? So for adult conversation because I had three boys under the age of five and I had been a professional and then all of a sudden I was at home and no one was listening to me. And I was isolated and we had moved to a new place and I just had lots of kids and they were needy. And I was sleep deprived and they woke up extremely early in the morning. And so I just like it was hard to get out of the house. And so I was emailing a friend every Saturday morning and for like a year that I had known in college like 10 years before and then all of a sudden she said, we're going to stop emailing. And I was like, no, it's like that's my only sad idea. She said, we're going to start blogs. I had no idea what that meant. She told me to go like start a blog, which I did because if I missed her information next week I would die. And like I told my mom about the blog and somebody else left the blog and I just started publishing about three weeks later I got a comment from somebody that wasn't those three people. How did you find this? And I clicked on her name. She had a blog. I was like not the only one in the world that blogged. And then she also like in those of you who've been on the internet for a long time, I mean she had a blog role in her sidebar and I like went crazy because I read through that and all those people in that blog are also my best friends. And so I found that community online and that's how that's what I want to kind of create through what we're doing here. We first started, do you start with the crafting or the kind of mold into that? No, so because I started with a blog called Doom Cleaver Nirvana because I was living a very traditional life but I had kind of everything was like a silver lining. So like I wrote tons of stories about just what happens in the suburbs of Dallas when you have three little boys. And then everything had a silver lining and then they got older and all of a sudden their teachers were reading and like it wasn't my story to tell anymore. So I had to really decide what to do with that as far as a conversation like what am I going to write about if I can't write about my boys. And so that's when I actually purchased quirkymom.com which was a kind of that like that simple thing to do with your kids, re-read it kids activities blog and then grew it from there. Our most of the moms who do this so they is because they want like it's because they're trying to like keep their kids out of their hair or is it because they feel guilty and they want to like be better moms. So they want to be better. So what I've seen over the last few years especially with Pinterest and like just like the almost like social media is like glossification of our everyday life. And so all of a sudden it tells a mom if you're not doing something pretty and photo worthy that it's not worth anything. And what like what that does is it puts like it puts emphasis on whatever they're creating like the end result. Whereas if you're doing a paper play craft with your two year old or three year old it's not about what it looks like at the end. It's more like you sat down and spent that time with them. And so that's kind of what we're trying to do is like show them and be supportive in that world of oh my gosh my sink is a total mess or yeah I didn't make the beds or the laundry's you know this tall. So when you're working through like the hook, get somebody to register for like a webinar for whatever it is. What things did you come up with so far? So like the origin story is one is just kind of like, I think a lot of moms like understand that like whole, like the weight of the world on your shoulders. And you know, and I think for me, it was, you know, because I had a career beforehand, and I had been working and had been very successful that, and I had always dreamed of being a stay at home mom. In fact, you know, it was my husband's a doctor. Like I was supporting our family until he finally got out. And so we had planned on having kids later, but like every morning before he went to work, I'm like, remember some day, you know, some day I'm going to, you know, you're going to be doing this and I'm going to be staying home. And then I get there and like I had had so much success beforehand. And then all of a sudden I'm like, leave in the kitchen like, what did I do? This is the dream. And but it is the dream. And I think that's such a hard thing is like, you have that conflict in your flow. Like I suck at this, but like that's, I mean, I have to get better at it because this is all my kids have. Like if I don't get better at this, like, you know, they're going to get therapy even longer. So like, I think, you know, talk about that. And then, and then like, I, a lot of the stories have to do with my oldest with the sensory issues. Like I didn't know what was going on with him. I thought he was bad. Like, and then I once I like started unlocking that, like, you know, his behavior is because he's overwhelmed or oh, his behavior is because he interprets things differently. And I think that I mean, like those videos are the ones that are most popular on my site because are the ones we show that when I'm like, like, because like when you have a kid that you can't understand your brain just goes to like, you know, either it's my fault or it's his fault. And neither one of those are good solutions. And so when you can say, you know what, likely he's doing the best he can and I'm doing the best I can, but there's something else that's, you know, kind of like the, you know, like, it's not our fault, which it really wasn't. I mean, we just had what's going on. That's cool. So yeah, like it's, you know, I literally took a paper plate and some scissors and we made paper plate roses. It's just really easy to make. And they love the second graders. It is mine. Oh man. What was the reason you transitioned to crafts again? It was because I it was about six years ago, five years ago when my boys were old enough to realize that I was writing about them and that like my oldest they were embarrassed about it. Well, yeah, they were embarrassed about it. And then also, like it's one thing to write about your two year old throwing a tantrum is another thing, writing about your nine year old and having his teacher read it or something like that. And like, you know, the head grown so large that it was like my kids, like we were in the park and they would get recognized. Yeah. And so they'll be like, in fact, oh my gosh, I was at the park one day and like one of my kids just ran off and one of the other moms I'd never seen before in my life said, oh, Holly, Ryan went that way. Okay. First I like ran over to get him and then I came back and I like, I mean, I had chills for about 30 minutes like what the heck just happened. And it was just a reader of my blog that knew not only my name, but even knew which of those kids was, you know, their names and all that kind of stuff. So that's when I was like, you know, maybe we need to like pull that back. Interesting. And then what, because I think you pick anything, but like crafts was, what was the reason that that was like, because you're not like a super craft. That wasn't like your favorite thing. No, but the way the site was set up and it was a fellow blogger there in the Dallas area and she, I mean, I could go on forever about this, but the short thing is, is I had met her once. She, I had seen her site and it was really cute. Like it was just super simple things that she was doing with her kids and she had four little kids and we, she asked me to come over. We had met at a blogger event and then she's like, Hey, come on over with the kids one day. So I did and I had been in her kitchen for about five minutes when she's like, I think I have to sell my blog. And I was like, I think I have to buy your blog before. And then I was like, Whoa, what did we just say? And I had already, the crazy thing is, is she was raising money for an international adoption and, and I had just sold a blog, actually brought my partners out of a blog that was exactly that same size. So I was like, this is what it's worth to me. Like, do you want it? And I was like, exactly what she was looking for for the adoption price. So we just exchanged that and I took it over. And then she ended up writing for it for several more years and she's gone on to amazing things in other areas. Cool. Very cool. All right. So when you typically talk to people about this, you talk to their moms and first come in, the fact that they can take this over, what's their biggest fear, what's the biggest thing that's why they don't go all in or you don't think they keep some from doing it right now? Yeah. So I have a ask campaign that let me grab some quotes. This one, I wrote down the full quote. I'll just read it to you because this is me and what I think is out there. She said, after my second was born, I became a stay at home mom and had all kinds of expectations of being the Pinterest mom, making crafts, goodies for school, being active in kids' school, making delicious, fancy home cooked meals instead of chicken nuggets and fries. Didn't realize I wouldn't have time, money, or energies for these. And she's so sad, but I'm sure she's a great mom. I mean, but how sad is that? That she thinks she's failed because she doesn't make something that she could photograph with on Pinterest. That sucks. That's awesome. Well, it sucks for her, but awesome. Somebody get hooks in there. Yeah. Yeah, you're kidding. Okay. That sounds useful. Are most women transitioning from like a career to a mother to motherhood? Is that a big part of it? It's really hard to tell, but I feel like it's a really big mix. And I don't know that this would ever be pigeonholed to one because it's, you want to use the time you have with your kids, whether you're working inside or outside the house or stay at home with your mom. Like you open like pottery barn kids and it's like these beautiful rooms and fun beds and like this big fluffy rug that you never have a child on. And then like... It's all white. It's all white. And then they have like one vintage train that's on its edge with the pull string like strewn across the floor and like, you know, like two inches of Legos would be way more like realistic. And nobody's ever seen the bedspread on any of my kids, you know. And I think like when we look at that all the time, I just... I think it... I mean, I think it makes us crazy. Okay. Like you could get up three hours earlier, but you need your sleep. I mean, like time is always, I think, the biggest commodity. Which is funny because like the days are so freaking long. I know what you're talking about. Saturdays? Saturdays I'm home with my kids all day. Yeah, we're talking about that. But like Saturday night, I'm like twitching. I'm just like, I just need to get back to the office. I'm just like, this is going to die. I love them so much. But, oh. Exactly. That's true. In the end of the day, please. Five more hours to bedtime. Four more hours to bedtime. Four more hours to bedtime. Three more hours. I remember going from one kid. Where I was like, I don't have time to do anything. And then the having two, and I was like, oh one is not a problem. But it's like you're trying to do laundry and dishes and stuff, and I'm like, I don't want to do it. I just want to just do it. And then I went and like... And I was like, I just want to go take a walk. I just wanted to do this. I just wanted to do this. I'm just going to do a walk. going from one kid where I was like, I don't have time to do anything. And then they're having two. And I was like, oh, one's not a problem. But it's like you're trying to do laundry and dishes and cook and crafts and Pinterest and Facebook and all the things. And so if it's like how to be the best mom ever in much time, I love that because the truth is the reason why we get into that spiral is that we don't sit down for five minutes with our kids. Because if we sat down for five minutes with our kids, they would be sick of us and run away. I mean, especially the older kids. But if there was that anchor of already spending time with the kid earlier on in the day, you would be feeling guilty about it like the rest of the day. The other thing that I heard when I was pregnant with my third was that I've tried to Google this. It doesn't exist. Someone totally made this up. But kids need 17 hugs a day. And so one of the things that we talk about is like, so you totally, let's say you don't meet your goal, but you've got three extra hugs or five extra hugs, how cool that would be. And then I tell the story about how I have boys. And so they're so not into the 17 hugs a day and by the end of the day. Stop touching me mom. I'm like, number 16. I'm totally chasing them around. My mom is so weird. I am the weirdest mom. I'm so uncool. But that has become that inside joke. And then I don't feel guilty at the end of the day, because when they walk into the room and I don't want them around, I'm like, it's time for a hug. And they're like, no. What is it that has 17 hugs a day can change your relationship with your kids forever? Forever. I love that. I'm totally having my kids. She said it wasn't true.
